title: A Big Fight Over Who Helps Kids with Disabilities at School
summary: Imagine if the people who make sure you're treated fairly at school suddenly lost their jobs. That's what's happening at the U.S. Department of Education. On Tuesday, Secretary Linda McMahon went to Capitol Hill to explain her plans. Senators from both parties had tough questions. One big fight is about special education. The government wants to spend more money on it—$16 billion—but also wants to move the program to a different department, like Health and Human Services. Many parents and advocates are "spitting mad," said Senator Patty Murray. They worry that if special education is treated like a health issue, kids might not get the same focus on learning. Another hot topic: the Office for Civil Rights, which investigates when students face discrimination. Last year, more than half its lawyers were fired, and seven of 12 local offices shut down. Senator Chris Murphy told the story of a student with autism who was restrained at school—her case was dropped when the office closed. McMahon denied responsibility for the cuts, calling it "hindsight," and promised to hire more lawyers. But the proposed budget still slashes $49 million from the office. The hearing got fiery, with McMahon insisting parents will "still get the same treatment" no matter where programs end up. But for many families, that promise feels shaky.
why_it_matters: If you or a friend ever need special help at school, decisions like these could affect whether you get the support you need to learn and feel safe.

questions.question: Who is the U.S. Secretary of Education?
Where did Secretary McMahon go to explain her plans?
How much money does the government want to spend on special education?
Which department might take over special education instead of the Department of Education?
What happened to many of the lawyers at the Office for Civil Rights?
How much money would be cut from the Office for Civil Rights under the proposed budget?

background_read: The U.S. Department of Education is in charge of schools and making sure all kids are treated fairly. Secretary Linda McMahon is the boss of that department. The Office for Civil Rights is a part of the department that looks into complaints about discrimination, like when a student with a disability is not given the right help. Special education programs have been required by law since 1975 to support students with learning challenges.
Article_Structure: WHO: Secretary Linda McMahon, senators from both parties, parents of children with disabilities, and student advocates.

WHAT: McMahon presented plans to spend $16 billion on special education but move it to the Department of Health and Human Services, while also cutting the Office for Civil Rights by $49 million and closing many offices.
WHERE: At a Senate hearing on Capitol Hill.

WHY: Some believe special education could be run differently, but many parents and senators worry that treating it as a health issue will weaken schools’ responsibility to teach students with disabilities.

perspectives.description: She believes moving special education to a different department will still treat families the same, and it’s about better organization.
They are 'spitting mad' and worry that treating special education as a health issue could mean schools pay less attention to learning and more to medical concerns.
Senators like Patty Murray and Chris Murphy brought up real stories of students hurt by office closures, doubting the promise that families will get the same treatment.
